posts.title: Agents that actually work look boring
posts.hook: The AI agents that are actually working in production right now look nothing like the demos on Twitter.
posts.body:
The AI agents that are actually working in production right now look nothing like the demos on Twitter.

They're not having conversations. They're not reasoning through complex chains. They're doing one thing, reliably, thousands of times a day.

Enriching a lead record with 4 data sources and writing the result back to your CRM. Parsing an inbound email, matching it to an account, routing it to the right rep. Scraping a job board, scoring the posting against your ICP, adding it to a campaign.

Boring stuff. Unsexy stuff. Stuff that used to take an SDR 45 minutes per account.

The gap between "agent demo" and "agent in production" is almost entirely about error handling. What happens when the API returns garbage. What happens when the LLM hallucinates a company name. What happens when your enrichment source has a 3 second timeout.

I've shipped maybe 15 of these workflows in the last 6 months. The ones that survived all share the same trait... they do less than I originally planned. Smaller scope. Fewer decisions. More guardrails.

The best agent architecture right now isn't a graph of reasoning nodes. It's a deterministic pipeline with one or two LLM calls where pattern matching actually matters.

Ship the boring version first. You can make it smart later.
posts.cta: What's the most boring automation you've shipped that saves the most time?

remixes.label: anti-demo-culture
remixes.hook: The flashiest AI demo I ever saw took 6 months to get into production. The ugliest one took 2 days.
remixes.body:
The flashiest AI demo I ever saw took 6 months to get into production. The ugliest one took 2 days.

The 6-month demo had a beautiful conversational interface, multi-step reasoning, and a slick UI. It impressed everyone in the boardroom. Then reality hit. Edge cases. Latency. Hallucinations that corrupted CRM data. The team spent months patching holes that didn't exist in the demo environment.

The 2-day build? A Python script triggered by a webhook. When a new lead hits the CRM, it calls three enrichment APIs in sequence, merges the results, scores the account against an ICP filter, and writes the output back. No UI. No conversation. No reasoning chain.

It processes about 400 leads a day and hasn't needed a code change in two months.

The gap between AI demos and AI in production isn't a technology problem. It's a scope problem. Demos optimize for impressions. Production optimizes for reliability.

Every team I've worked with that shipped a working agent started with the same question: what's the smallest unit of work we can automate end-to-end? Not the most impressive. The smallest.

That constraint kills complexity before it starts. And complexity is what kills agents in production.
remixes.cta: What's the simplest agent you've shipped that's still running without issues?

remixes.label: reliability-over-intelligence
remixes.hook: The word "intelligent" appears in zero of my production agent descriptions. The word "reliable" appears in all of them.
remixes.body:
The word "intelligent" appears in zero of my production agent descriptions. The word "reliable" appears in all of them.

I run about a dozen automated workflows across client GTM stacks. Lead enrichment. Email parsing. Account scoring. Slack notifications with context. None of them are smart. All of them are consistent.

Here's what I've learned matters in production agent work:

Idempotency. If the same input hits twice, the output doesn't break anything. This sounds obvious until you realize most "AI agent" tutorials never mention it.

Fallback paths. When the LLM returns garbage (and it will), what happens? If the answer is "the workflow stops," you don't have a production system. You have a prototype.

Observability. Every run logs input, output, latency, and cost. Not because something's broken today, but because you need to know the moment it drifts.

These aren't exciting topics. They don't get engagement on social media. But they're the difference between an agent that works in a demo and one that runs for months without anyone thinking about it.

The best agents disappear into the workflow. Nobody talks about them because there's nothing to talk about. They just work.
remixes.cta: What reliability patterns have you found essential for keeping agents running in production?

: most teams don't need an AI agent. They need a well-built automation with an LLM call in the middle. The "agent" framing is marketing. The value is in the boring plumbing around it.

posts.title: GPU costs are a GTM problem
posts.hook: Everyone's talking about GPU costs as an infrastructure problem. It's a GTM problem.
posts.body:
Everyone's talking about GPU costs as an infrastructure problem. It's a GTM problem.

If you're running AI enrichment on every lead that hits your CRM, you're burning compute on accounts that will never close. I watched a team spend $1,200/month on GPT-4 API calls to write personalized emails to leads that had a 0.3% reply rate.

The fix wasn't better prompts. It was better targeting.

When you filter your enrichment pipeline to only run on accounts that match 3+ ICP signals before the AI touches them, your costs drop 80% and your output quality goes up. Because you're not asking the model to personalize for a company you know nothing about.

This is the same lesson from outbound, just applied to compute. Volume is not a strategy. Precision is.

The teams I see winning right now run a cheap deterministic filter first. Firmographics, technographics, hiring signals. All structured data. No LLM needed. Then the AI layer only activates on the 15-20% that actually look like buyers.

Your AI bill is a reflection of your targeting discipline. Not your engineering.
posts.cta: How are you managing AI/API costs in your pipeline?

remixes.label: unit-economics-lens
remixes.hook: We calculated the cost-per-meeting for our AI-enriched pipeline last quarter. The number was embarrassing.
remixes.body:
We calculated the cost-per-meeting for our AI-enriched pipeline last quarter. The number was embarrassing.

$47 per meeting booked. Sounds reasonable until you realize $31 of that was API costs for enrichment and personalization on leads that never responded.

The breakdown: every inbound lead triggered a 4-step enrichment flow. Company data from one provider, contact info from another, technographics from a third, then a GPT-4 call to write a personalized first line. Total cost per lead: about $0.85.

At 1,200 leads per month, that's $1,020 just in enrichment. Our reply rate was 3.2%. Our meeting conversion from reply was 41%. So we booked about 16 meetings. $1,020 divided by 16 is $63.75 per meeting just in API costs. Before you count the tools, the reps, the SDR time.

The fix was embarrassingly simple. We added an ICP filter before the enrichment step. A basic scoring model using firmographic data we already had. Company size, industry, tech stack signals from the website.

Filtered out 68% of leads before they hit the enrichment pipeline. API costs dropped to $326/month. Meeting volume dropped by only 2 because the filtered leads weren't converting anyway.

Cost-per-meeting went from $63.75 to $21.70 in API spend. The AI didn't get smarter. The targeting did.
remixes.cta: Have you calculated your actual cost-per-meeting including API and enrichment spend?

remixes.label: waste-audit-framework
remixes.hook: Run this audit on your AI pipeline spend. Takes 15 minutes. Might save you thousands per month.
remixes.body:
Run this audit on your AI pipeline spend. Takes 15 minutes. Might save you thousands per month.

Step 1: Pull your API usage logs for the last 30 days. Every enrichment call, every LLM generation, every scoring request. Total the cost.

Step 2: Match each API call back to the lead it processed. How many of those leads converted to a reply? A meeting? A deal?

Step 3: Calculate cost per outcome at each stage. Cost per enriched lead. Cost per reply. Cost per meeting. Cost per deal.

When I run this for teams, the pattern is almost always the same. 60-80% of API spend goes to leads that never engage. Not because the AI failed. Because the lead was never a fit.

The root cause is architectural. Most teams set up their automation as: lead enters CRM, trigger enrichment, trigger personalization, trigger outreach. Every lead gets the full treatment.

The fix is a filter before the expensive steps. Use the cheap data you already have (company size, industry, title, basic firmographics) to score before you spend on enrichment.

This isn't an AI optimization. It's a targeting optimization that happens to save AI costs. The best dollar you save is the one you never spend on a lead that was never going to buy.
remixes.cta: When's the last time you audited your pipeline's API spend against actual conversions?

posts.title: No-code agents need an operator, not a user
posts.hook: No-code AI agents are real now. But the people succeeding with them aren't "non-technical." They're operationally obsessive.
posts.body:
No-code AI agents are real now. But the people succeeding with them aren't "non-technical." They're operationally obsessive.

I've set up Clay tables, n8n workflows, and Make scenarios for teams that range from 2-person startups to 50-person sales orgs. The pattern is consistent.

The tools work. The problem is always the operator.

You don't need to write code to build a lead enrichment agent. But you need to understand what "enrichment" actually means for your pipeline. Which fields matter. What order to waterfall your data sources. How to handle conflicts when ZoomInfo says one thing and Apollo says another.

That's not a technical skill. That's an operational skill. And most teams don't have it.

The best no-code agent builders I've seen are former SDRs and ops people who spent years doing the work manually. They know every edge case because they lived them. They know which data sources lie about employee count. They know that "Director" in a 50-person company is not the same as "Director" at Oracle.

The tool democratization is real. The knowledge gap isn't closing as fast.

If you're building no-code agents, pair your tools person with someone who's done the job by hand. That combination is unbeatable.
posts.cta: Who on your team is the best fit to own agent workflows?

inspired_by.author: Nat Friedman
inspired_by.summary: Analyzes the rise of no-code AI agents for business automation, showcasing tools that enable non-developers to build custom workflows. Warns about over-reliance on black-box agents and advocates for hybrid human-AI systems. Includes case studies from early adopters in sales and marketing.

remixes.label: operator-archetype
remixes.hook: The best no-code agent builder I've met is a former restaurant manager. That's not a coincidence.
remixes.body:
The best no-code agent builder I've met is a former restaurant manager. That's not a coincidence.

She ran a 40-seat restaurant with 12 staff. She knew exactly which tasks could be systematized, which decisions needed a human, and which processes broke under pressure. When she started building Clay and Make workflows, she applied the same mental models.

This is the pattern I keep seeing. The people who succeed with no-code AI agents aren't technical. They're operational. They think in systems, not features.

What makes a great agent operator:

They map the full workflow before they touch the tool. Every input, output, exception case, and handoff point. Most people start clicking buttons in Clay or n8n before they've drawn the workflow on paper.

They define "done" precisely. Not "enrich the lead" but "write company size, funding stage, and tech stack to these three CRM fields, and if any data is missing, flag it in this Slack channel."

They test with real data on day one. Not sample data. Not demo data. The messy, inconsistent, sometimes-broken data that actually flows through their system.

They build monitoring before they build features. They know what "working" looks like in metrics before they ship.

The tools are genuinely powerful now. Clay, Make, n8n, Zapier with AI steps. You can build real automation without writing code. But "no-code" doesn't mean "no skill." It means the skill shifted from programming to operations.
remixes.cta: What operational background has been most useful for your team's automation work?

remixes.label: failure-pattern-catalog
remixes.hook: I've watched 20+ no-code agent projects fail. Same three mistakes every time.
remixes.body:
I've watched 20+ no-code agent projects fail. Same three mistakes every time.

Mistake 1: Building for the happy path only.

The workflow works perfectly with clean data. Then a lead comes in with a missing email. Or a company name that doesn't match any enrichment database. Or a phone number in the wrong format. The workflow breaks silently. Data gets corrupted. Nobody notices for weeks.

The fix: design for the messy cases first. What happens when data is missing? When an API returns an error? When a field has an unexpected format? Build those branches before the golden path.

Mistake 2: No ownership after launch.

Someone builds the workflow, shows it off in a team meeting, and moves on to the next project. Two months later, an API changes its response format. The workflow starts writing bad data to the CRM. Nobody's watching.

The fix: assign an owner, not a builder. Someone who checks the logs weekly, monitors the error rates, and updates the workflow when upstream systems change.

Mistake 3: Automating a broken process.

If your manual process has inconsistent rules, unclear handoffs, and tribal knowledge, automating it doesn't fix it. It scales the problems. Before building the agent, document the process. Find the gaps. Fix the logic. Then automate.

No-code AI tools are powerful. But they amplify whatever you point them at, including your operational debt.
remixes.cta: Which of these mistakes have you seen (or made) with no-code automation?

No-code AI agents are genuinely capable now. But I'd caution against equating "no-code" with "easy" or "anyone can do it."

The teams I've worked with, from 2-person startups to 50-person sales orgs, show a consistent pattern. The tools work. The bottleneck is always the operator.

You don't need to write code to build a lead enrichment agent. But you do need to understand your data model. You need to know what happens when an enrichment API returns null. You need to think about what "success" looks like in measurable terms, not just "it runs."

My recommendation for teams evaluating no-code agent tools: before you buy a single license, identify the person who will own these workflows. Not build them once. Own them ongoing.

The ideal profile isn't a developer. It's someone who thinks in systems. They map workflows end-to-end before touching the tool. They define precise success criteria. They build monitoring before features. They test with real data, not samples.

Operationally obsessive people succeed with these tools regardless of technical background. Everyone else builds something that works in the demo and breaks in production within a month.
tones.provocateur:
No-code AI agents are a trap if you don't understand what "no-code" actually eliminated.

It eliminated the syntax. The semicolons, the deployment configs, the dependency management. It did not eliminate the thinking. And the thinking is where every failed automation dies.

I've set up Clay tables, n8n workflows, and Make scenarios for teams across the spectrum. The pattern is brutal in its consistency. The tools work. The people don't.

Not because they're dumb. Because they think "no-code" means "no expertise required." It doesn't. It means the expertise shifted. Instead of needing a developer who understands APIs, you need an operator who understands systems.

You need someone who asks: what happens when this field is empty? What happens when this API is down? What does "working correctly" look like in numbers I can check every Monday?

Most teams skip all of this. They build the happy path, demo it to leadership, and move on. Six weeks later the workflow is silently writing bad data to the CRM and nobody knows.

The hard truth: if your team doesn't have someone who is operationally obsessive, no-code agents will create more problems than they solve. The tool isn't the bottleneck. The operator gap is.

posts.title: Edge functions changed my agent architecture
posts.hook: Moved three agent workflows from a central server to edge functions last month. Changed how I think about agent architecture entirely.
posts.body:
Moved three agent workflows from a central server to edge functions last month. Changed how I think about agent architecture entirely.

The workflows: webhook receiver for inbound leads, real-time ICP scoring on form submissions, and a Slack bot that pulls account context on demand.

All three used to run on a single Node server. Worked fine until the webhook volume spiked and the scoring latency went from 200ms to 4 seconds. Slack bot started timing out.

Edge deployment fixed the latency issue, but the real insight was about architecture. When your agent runs at the edge, you're forced to make it stateless. No long-running processes. No in-memory caches. Every invocation is independent.

That constraint made the workflows better. Each function does exactly one thing. State lives in the database. Failures are isolated. I can update the scoring logic without touching the webhook handler.

The old architecture was a monolith pretending to be microservices. Three workflows sharing a process, sharing memory, sharing failure modes.

Edge functions aren't just a deployment target. They're an architectural discipline. They force you to build agents the way agents should be built... small, stateless, and independently deployable.

Not everything belongs at the edge. But if your agent workflow is request/response shaped, it probably does.
posts.cta: Anyone else moving agent workloads to edge? What's your stack look like?

inspired_by.author: Guillermo Rauch
inspired_by.summary: Dives into developer tools for AI-native applications, introducing Vercel's new edge runtime for agentic workloads. Covers performance benchmarks comparing serverless vs. dedicated AI infra. Predicts agent swarms will redefine web development paradigms.

remixes.label: cost-latency-tradeoff
remixes.hook: My agent infrastructure costs dropped 60% last month. The change had nothing to do with optimizing code.
remixes.body:
My agent infrastructure costs dropped 60% last month. The change had nothing to do with optimizing code.

I moved three workflows off a central Node server and onto Cloudflare Workers. The workflows: a webhook receiver for inbound leads, real-time ICP scoring on form submissions, and a Slack bot that pulls account context on demand.

The cost math is simple. A $20/month VPS running 24/7 to handle workloads that are active maybe 3% of the time. Edge functions bill per invocation. When nothing fires, I pay nothing.

But cost wasn't even the biggest win. Latency was.

The ICP scoring workflow used to take 800ms on average because every request traveled to a central server in Virginia. After moving to edge, the scoring function runs in the region closest to the user. Average latency dropped to 120ms. For a real-time form submission flow, that difference is visible to the end user.

The webhook receiver was the easiest migration. Stateless by design. Receives payload, validates it, transforms it, pushes to a queue. No database connection needed during the hot path.

The Slack bot was harder. It needed account context, which meant a database read. Solution: cache the most-accessed accounts in KV storage at the edge. Cache hit rate is about 85%, so most requests never touch the origin database.

Not everything belongs on the edge. Long-running enrichment jobs, batch processing, anything that needs a persistent connection. Those stay on the server. But for event-driven, stateless agent workloads, edge functions are a better default.
remixes.cta: What workloads have you moved to edge, and what did you keep centralized?

remixes.label: architecture-decision-record
remixes.hook: Here's the decision framework I use to determine if an agent workflow belongs on edge or on a server.
remixes.body:
Here's the decision framework I use to determine if an agent workflow belongs on edge or on a server.

Three questions:

1. Is the workflow stateless or can state be externalized? If the function needs to read and write to a database on every invocation, edge adds complexity without much benefit. If it's stateless, or state lives in a KV store or queue, edge works well.

2. Is latency user-facing? Background enrichment jobs can take 2 seconds and nobody cares. A form submission scorer that takes 2 seconds feels broken. If the latency matters to a human, edge matters.

3. Is the traffic pattern bursty or consistent? Edge functions handle burst traffic naturally because there's no server to overwhelm. If your webhook volume can spike 10x in an hour (product launch, campaign blast), edge handles it without provisioning.

I applied this framework to three agent workflows last month:

Webhook receiver for inbound leads: stateless, not user-facing, bursty traffic. Moved to edge. Cost dropped from $20/month server allocation to about $3/month in invocations.

ICP scoring on form submissions: externalized state (model weights in KV), user-facing latency, moderate traffic. Moved to edge. Latency went from 800ms to 120ms.

Slack bot for account context: needed database reads, user-facing, low traffic. Partial edge. Hot path cached in KV, cold path falls through to origin. 85% of requests served from edge.

The pattern: default to edge for event-driven agent workloads. Keep the server for batch jobs and persistent connections.
remixes.cta: What criteria do you use to decide where a workflow runs?

tones.builder:
Moved three agent workflows from a central server to edge functions last month. Changed how I think about agent architecture entirely.

The workflows: webhook receiver for inbound leads, real-time ICP scoring on form submissions, and a Slack bot that pulls account context on demand.

All three used to run on a single Node server. Worked fine until the webhook volume spiked and the scoring latency went from 200ms to 4 seconds. Slack bot started timing out.

Edge deployment fixed the latency issue, but the real insight was about architecture. When your agent runs at the edge, you're forced to make it stateless. No long-running processes. No in-memory caches. Every invocation is independent.

That constraint made the workflows better. Each function does exactly one thing. State lives in the database. Failures are isolated. I can update the scoring logic without touching the webhook handler.

The old architecture was a monolith pretending to be microservices. Three workflows sharing a process, sharing memory, sharing failure modes.

Edge functions aren't just a deployment target. They're an architectural discipline. They force you to build agents the way agents should be built... small, stateless, and independently deployable.

Not everything belongs at the edge. But if your agent workflow is request/response shaped, it probably does.

posts.title: GTM engineering isn't new, it's renamed
posts.hook: "GTM Engineer" is trending as a new role. It's not new. It's what good sales ops people have been doing for years, except now they have better tools.
posts.body:
"GTM Engineer" is trending as a new role. It's not new. It's what good sales ops people have been doing for years, except now they have better tools.

The best rev ops person I ever worked with built a lead routing system in Salesforce using flows, Process Builder, and a Google Sheet as a config layer. It was ugly. It worked perfectly. It replaced two SDRs worth of manual routing.

That was GTM engineering. She just didn't call it that.

What's actually different now is the tooling layer. Clay, n8n, Make, custom scripts. The ceiling on what one ops person can automate went from "Salesforce flows" to "anything with an API." That's a real shift.

But the core skill hasn't changed. You need someone who:

- Understands the sales process end to end
- Can translate business logic into system logic
- Knows when to automate and when to leave it manual
- Can debug a broken workflow at 2am when pipeline is on the line

The title change matters because it signals to companies that this role needs engineering-level investment. Real tools. Real infrastructure. Not just admin access to Salesforce.

If you're hiring for this role, look at your best ops person first. They already know your pipeline. Give them the tools and get out of the way.
posts.cta: What's the most impactful automation your ops team has built?

inspired_by.author: Sami Rahman
inspired_by.summary: Discusses building scalable GTM engines by integrating engineering practices into go-to-market strategies. Emphasizes data-driven experimentation and automation to accelerate revenue growth. Shares a framework for aligning product, sales, and marketing teams.

remixes.label: skills-transfer-angle
remixes.hook: The best GTM engineer I know has never written a line of JavaScript. She came from revenue operations.
remixes.body:
The best GTM engineer I know has never written a line of JavaScript. She came from revenue operations.

She built lead scoring models in spreadsheets before anyone called it "AI-powered ICP scoring." She automated handoffs between SDRs and AEs using Salesforce flows when the rest of the team was doing it manually in Slack. She connected data sources that the engineering team didn't even know existed.

The title is new. The work isn't.

What changed is the tooling. Five years ago, building a lead routing system required either an expensive consultant or a rev ops person who could hack together Salesforce flows, Zapier, and Google Sheets. It was fragile. It took weeks. It broke when someone renamed a field.

Now you can build the same system in Clay or n8n in an afternoon. The logic is the same. The interface is better. The maintenance burden is lower.

But here's what hasn't changed: you still need someone who understands the business logic. What makes a lead qualified. When a handoff should happen. Which data signals actually predict conversion versus which ones just feel important.

That knowledge lives in rev ops. It always has. The "GTM Engineer" title is just the industry recognizing that this operational knowledge, combined with better tools, is a distinct and valuable skill set.

If you're hiring for a "GTM Engineer" role and only looking at software engineering backgrounds, you're fishing in the wrong pond. Look at your ops team. The person who already understands your pipeline is more valuable than someone who can write Python but doesn't know your ICP.
remixes.cta: Are you seeing GTM engineer roles filled more by ops backgrounds or engineering backgrounds?

remixes.label: tools-changed-title-didnt
remixes.hook: I found a job posting from 2019 for "Senior Revenue Operations Manager." The responsibilities are identical to today's "GTM Engineer" postings.
remixes.body:
I found a job posting from 2019 for "Senior Revenue Operations Manager." The responsibilities are identical to today's "GTM Engineer" postings.

2019 posting: "Design and maintain lead scoring, routing, and enrichment workflows. Build automated handoffs between sales stages. Connect CRM to marketing automation and outbound tools. Report on pipeline metrics."

2026 posting for "GTM Engineer": "Design and maintain AI-powered lead scoring, routing, and enrichment workflows. Build automated handoffs between sales stages. Connect CRM to enrichment APIs and outbound tools. Report on pipeline metrics."

Spot the difference. I'll wait.

The word "AI-powered" was added. The APIs changed. The job didn't.

This isn't a criticism. The tooling evolution is real and significant. Building a lead enrichment pipeline with Clay and Anthropic's API is genuinely faster and more capable than doing it with Clearbit and Zapier in 2019. The outputs are better. The iteration cycles are shorter.

But the underlying skill is the same: understanding what data matters, how it should flow, and what actions it should trigger. That's operational thinking. It predates AI.

The risk with the "GTM Engineer" title is that it attracts people who know the new tools but don't understand the business logic underneath. You end up with beautifully engineered pipelines that score leads on the wrong criteria.

The best GTM engineers I've worked with started in ops. They learned the tools to serve the logic they already understood, not the other way around.
remixes.cta: Is your team treating GTM engineering as a new discipline or an evolution of rev ops?

posts.title: Over-automated outbound is dead
posts.hook: I've seen 6 outbound sequences this week that were clearly written by AI, sent by AI, and will be deleted by a human without reading.
posts.body:
I've seen 6 outbound sequences this week that were clearly written by AI, sent by AI, and will be deleted by a human without reading.

The irony of outbound automation in 2026: the tools got so good that everyone's using them the same way. Same enrichment sources. Same personalization templates. Same "I noticed your company recently..." opener.

When I was an SDR sending 200+ emails a day from primary domains with no warmup on SalesLoft, the emails were bad but at least they were different. Every rep had their own terrible style. Now every email sounds like the same competent robot.

The play that's working right now is using AI for research, not writing.

Pull the 10-K. Scan their job postings. Read their last 3 blog posts. Find the specific initiative they're investing in. Then write the email yourself in 90 seconds because you actually know what to say.

The automation layer should make you informed, not eloquent. An ugly email with a sharp insight beats a polished email with generic personalization every time.

I'm running this exact workflow in Clay right now. 4 enrichment columns feed a research brief. Zero AI-written email copy. Reply rates are 3x what they were when I let the model write the emails.

Automate the research. Write the message.
posts.cta: What's your split between AI-researched and AI-written outbound?

inspired_by.author: Josh Braun
inspired_by.summary: Explains how outbound automation tools can enhance personalization without losing the human touch. Warns against over-automation leading to spam and shares tips for compliant, high-response sequences. Highlights case studies from B2B SaaS companies.

remixes.label: buyer-perspective-flip
remixes.hook: I deleted 11 cold emails before 9am today. Every single one mentioned something "I noticed" about my company.
remixes.body:
I deleted 11 cold emails before 9am today. Every single one mentioned something "I noticed" about my company.

Here's what buyers actually experience in 2026: a wall of AI-generated personalization that all sounds identical. "I saw you recently raised a Series B." "Congrats on the new VP of Sales hire." "I noticed your company is expanding into EMEA."

Yes, you noticed. So did the other 40 people who pulled the same data from the same enrichment tool and ran it through the same prompt template.

The emails I actually reply to? They reference something specific I said in a podcast. They connect my problem to a result they got for someone in my exact situation. They sound like a person wrote them because a person did write them, after spending 10 minutes actually thinking about my business.

The math has flipped. When AI outbound was rare, volume won. Now that everyone has volume, the signal is effort. The SDR who sends 30 researched emails outperforms the one sending 300 templated ones.

Automation should handle the research and logistics. The actual message needs a human fingerprint.
remixes.cta: Buyers: what's the last cold email you actually responded to, and why?

remixes.label: tactical-breakdown
remixes.hook: My outbound reply rate doubled when I stopped letting AI write the emails and started letting it do everything else.
remixes.body:
My outbound reply rate doubled when I stopped letting AI write the emails and started letting it do everything else.

Here's the workflow that actually works:

Step 1: AI scans target accounts, pulls recent news, job postings, tech stack signals, and leadership changes. Pure research. No writing.

Step 2: AI clusters the signals into a one-paragraph briefing per account. "This company just hired 3 AEs, posted a RevOps role, and their CEO talked about pipeline problems on a podcast last week."

Step 3: I read the briefing and write the email myself. 4 sentences. Specific reference. Clear connection to what I do. No template.

Step 4: AI handles the send timing, follow-up cadence, and CRM logging.

The result: 30 emails a day instead of 200. Triple the reply rate. Zero "I noticed your company recently" openers.

The mistake most teams make is automating the wrong step. They automate the writing, which is the one part that needs to feel human. They leave the research manual, which is the part AI is genuinely better at.

Flip the script. Let AI do the boring work. Keep the creative work human.
remixes.cta: What step of your outbound process is still manual that shouldn't be?

posts.title: Ship weekly or you're not building
posts.hook: Shipped 4 internal tools in the last 4 weeks. None of them took more than a week. None of them are "done."
posts.body:
Shipped 4 internal tools in the last 4 weeks. None of them took more than a week. None of them are "done."

A lead scoring calculator that pulls from 3 APIs. A daily brief that summarizes pipeline changes overnight. A content scheduler that posts to LinkedIn via API. A prospect research agent that generates account briefs.

All of them are rough. All of them work. All of them save me hours every week.

The solo builder advantage isn't talent. It's cycle time. No PRDs. No design reviews. No sprint planning. Idea on Monday, deployed by Thursday, iterating by Friday.

The trap is thinking you need to build something complete before it's useful. My lead scorer started as a single API call with hardcoded weights. The daily brief was literally a cron job that ran a SQL query and dumped it into a text file. Both evolved from there.

V1 should embarrass you slightly. If it doesn't, you spent too long on it.

The builders I respect most right now aren't shipping polished products. They're shipping rough tools that solve real problems, learning from usage, and iterating fast. The polish comes from contact with reality, not from planning.

Stop architecting. Start shipping. The feedback from real usage teaches you more than any amount of planning.
posts.cta: What's something you shipped this week that's rough but working?

inspired_by.author: Pieter Levels
inspired_by.summary: Pieter shared his latest product launch, a new tool for remote workers built entirely solo in a week. He detailed the tech stack, challenges faced during development, and initial user feedback. Emphasizing building in public, he invited feedback to iterate quickly.

remixes.label: anti-perfectionism
remixes.hook: The best tools I've built this year would embarrass me if anyone saw the code. They also save me 15+ hours a week.
remixes.body:
The best tools I've built this year would embarrass me if anyone saw the code. They also save me 15+ hours a week.

One of them crashes if you pass it a date in the wrong format. Another one has hardcoded API keys in the script (yes, I know). A third one outputs results to a plain text file because I never got around to building a proper UI.

They're all ugly. They all work. And they've compounded into a system that runs my entire operation.

There's a specific trap that kills solo builders: the urge to make something "production ready" before it's proven useful. You spend three weeks building auth, error handling, and a beautiful dashboard for a tool you'll abandon in a month.

The pattern I follow instead: build the smallest version that does the job. Use it daily. If it keeps being useful after two weeks, invest in making it better. If not, delete it and move on.

Four of my current daily-use tools started as throwaway scripts. Two of them are still throwaway scripts. They just happen to be throwaway scripts that save me hours every day.

Perfection is the enemy of leverage. Ship the ugly thing.
remixes.cta: What's the ugliest tool you rely on daily?

remixes.label: compound-leverage
remixes.hook: Week 1: built a tool that saves 30 minutes a day. Week 4: those tools combined save 3 hours a day. That's the math nobody talks about.
remixes.body:
Week 1: built a tool that saves 30 minutes a day. Week 4: those tools combined save 3 hours a day. That's the math nobody talks about.

Shipping fast isn't about speed for its own sake. It's about compound leverage.

The lead scorer I built in week 1 surfaces the right accounts. The daily brief I built in week 2 uses those scored accounts to prioritize my pipeline review. The research agent I built in week 3 pulls context on the top accounts from the brief. The content scheduler I built in week 4 posts about the patterns I'm seeing across those accounts.

Each tool is simple on its own. Together, they form an operating system for one person to do the work of a small team.

But here's the part that matters: none of this would exist if I'd tried to design the whole system upfront. I didn't have a master plan. I had a problem on Monday, I built a solution by Friday, and the next week's problem revealed itself.

The secret to building real leverage as a solo operator is shipping weekly and letting the system emerge from usage, not from architecture diagrams.

Plan less. Ship more. Let the workflow tell you what to build next.
remixes.cta: What tools have you stacked together into something bigger than the sum of parts?

tones.builder:
Shipped 4 internal tools in the last 4 weeks. None of them took more than a week. None of them are "done."

A lead scoring calculator that pulls from 3 APIs. A daily brief that summarizes pipeline changes overnight. A content scheduler that posts to LinkedIn via API. A prospect research agent that generates account briefs.

All of them are rough. All of them work. All of them save me hours every week.

The solo builder advantage isn't talent. It's cycle time. No PRDs. No design reviews. No sprint planning. Idea on Monday, deployed by Thursday, iterating by Friday.

The trap is thinking you need to build something complete before it's useful. My lead scorer started as a single API call with hardcoded weights. The daily brief was literally a cron job that ran a SQL query and dumped it into a text file. Both evolved from there.

V1 should embarrass you slightly. If it doesn't, you spent too long on it.

The builders I respect most right now aren't shipping polished products. They're shipping rough tools that solve real problems, learning from usage, and iterating fast. The polish comes from contact with reality, not from planning.

Stop architecting. Start shipping. The feedback from real usage teaches you more than any amount of planning.
tones.advisor:
There's a rhythm to building internal tools that separates operators who gain leverage from those who stay stuck in planning mode. Here's what I recommend.

Set a weekly shipping cadence. Not a monthly roadmap. Not a quarterly plan. One useful tool, every week, regardless of polish level.

The four tools I shipped recently follow a pattern worth copying. A lead scoring calculator pulling from 3 APIs. A daily pipeline brief that runs overnight. A content scheduler with LinkedIn API integration. A prospect research agent generating account briefs.

None took more than a week. None are finished. All of them work and save hours.

The framework: identify your biggest daily time sink on Monday. Build the minimum viable solution by Wednesday. Use it Thursday and Friday. Iterate the following week only if it proves useful.

What kills most internal tool projects is premature optimization. You don't need auth. You don't need a UI. You don't need error handling for edge cases that may never happen. You need a script that runs, produces useful output, and saves you time.

Ship rough. Use it. Refine only what earns refinement through daily use. That's how solo operators build real leverage.
tones.provocateur:
You spent 6 weeks planning your internal tool and it still isn't shipped. I built 4 in the same timeframe and they're already running my business.

This isn't a flex. It's an indictment of how most people think about building.

The industry has convinced everyone that tools need to be "production ready" before they're useful. That you need proper architecture, test coverage, CI/CD, and a Notion doc describing the system design.

You don't. You need a script that runs and gives you useful output. That's it.

My lead scoring tool is a Python script with hardcoded thresholds. My daily brief is a cron job that dumps data into a markdown file. My content scheduler is 200 lines of code that hits an API. My research agent is a prompt chain with some data piped in.

All of them are embarrassingly simple. All of them run daily. All of them compound into leverage that a perfectly planned, never-shipped system can never provide.

The gap between operators who build leverage and those who stay stuck isn't skill. It's the willingness to ship something ugly on Friday instead of something perfect never.

Stop planning. Start shipping. Refine later.

posts.title: RevOps unification is a trap
posts.hook: Hot take: unifying your entire tech stack under one platform is a trap disguised as efficiency.
posts.body:
Hot take: unifying your entire tech stack under one platform is a trap disguised as efficiency.

I've watched 3 teams this year try to consolidate everything into HubSpot or Salesforce. CRM, outbound, analytics, content, support. One platform to rule them all.

Every single one ended up with worse data, slower workflows, and a $40k/year platform bill for features they use at 30% capacity.

The problem with unification is that no platform is best at everything. HubSpot's email sequencing is fine. It's not Instantly. Salesforce's reporting is powerful. It's not Looker. Both of their enrichment is mediocre compared to purpose-built tools.

What actually works is a thin integration layer connecting best-in-class tools. Your CRM is your system of record. Everything else pushes and pulls from it via API or middleware.

My stack right now: Attio for CRM, Clay for enrichment, n8n for orchestration, Supabase for custom data, Typefully for content distribution. Total cost is less than a single HubSpot enterprise seat. Each tool does its job extremely well.

The integration work takes a weekend. The vendor lock-in savings last years.

Unify your data model, not your tools. Standard field names, consistent IDs, one source of truth for each data type. That's the real RevOps architecture.
posts.cta: Unified platform or best-of-breed stack? What are you running?

remixes.label: cost-autopsy
remixes.hook: A team I know spent $40k/year on HubSpot Enterprise. They use 30% of the features. The other 70% actively makes their workflow worse.
remixes.body:
A team I know spent $40k/year on HubSpot Enterprise. They use 30% of the features. The other 70% actively makes their workflow worse.

This is what happens when you buy into the "unified platform" pitch. You start with CRM. Then you add outbound because it's included. Then analytics. Then content. Then support. Each addition feels free because it's already in the platform.

But each addition comes with compromises. The outbound tools are fine, not great. The analytics are decent, not deep. The content management works, but it's rigid. You end up with a mediocre version of everything instead of a great version of anything.

Meanwhile, the data problems multiply. Custom objects that don't map cleanly. Reporting that requires workarounds. Integrations with external tools that break every time the platform updates.

The real cost isn't the subscription. It's the operational debt. Your team builds workflows around platform limitations instead of business needs. They spend hours on admin and workarounds that wouldn't exist with purpose-built tools.

I run a stack of 5 specialized tools connected through APIs. Total cost: under $200/month. Each tool is best-in-class for its specific job. When one underperforms, I swap it out in a day.

That's the flexibility a unified platform can never offer.
remixes.cta: What's the most expensive underused feature in your current stack?

remixes.label: integration-reality
remixes.hook: "But the integrations are native!" is the tech stack equivalent of "but it was on sale!"
remixes.body:
"But the integrations are native!" is the tech stack equivalent of "but it was on sale!"

Teams justify platform consolidation with the integration argument. Everything talks to everything. No middleware. No API management. Clean data flow.

In practice? Native integrations are often the most limited kind. They expose 60% of the functionality, lock you into the platform's data model, and break in subtle ways that are harder to debug than a straightforward API connection.

I've worked with teams running both approaches. The unified platform teams spend more time on workarounds, not less. They build custom fields to compensate for rigid schemas. They create manual processes to bridge gaps between modules. They hire platform specialists to manage complexity that shouldn't exist.

The best-of-breed teams have a different problem set. They manage integrations. But modern integration tools make this trivial. A simple API layer connecting your CRM, outbound tool, analytics, and content system takes a day to build and an hour a month to maintain.

The dirty secret of platform consolidation: you're not reducing complexity. You're moving it from visible integration work into hidden operational compromises that cost you more in the long run.

Pick the best tool for each job. Connect them with simple APIs. Own your data model instead of renting someone else's.
remixes.cta: What's one "native integration" that turned out to be more trouble than a simple API call?

I'd strongly caution against the platform consolidation trend that's taken hold in RevOps. Here's what I've seen play out repeatedly.

Teams consolidate into HubSpot or Salesforce with the goal of simplifying their stack. CRM, outbound, analytics, content, support, all under one roof. The pitch sounds compelling: fewer tools, cleaner data, native integrations.

The reality is different. I've watched three teams do this in the past year. Every one ended up with degraded data quality, slower workflows, and annual platform costs in the $30-50k range for features they barely touch.

Here's what I recommend instead. Keep your CRM as the system of record. Choose best-in-class tools for outbound, analytics, and content individually. Connect them through a simple API layer or a lightweight integration tool.

The total cost is typically 40-60% lower. Each tool does its specific job better than a platform module. And when a tool underperforms, you swap it in days instead of migrating an entire platform.

The key principle: optimize for flexibility and data ownership, not for the illusion of simplicity. A well-connected stack of 4-5 specialized tools will outperform a monolithic platform every time. The integration overhead is real but manageable, and it's a far smaller cost than the operational compromises of consolidation.
tones.provocateur:
The "unified platform" pitch is the enterprise software equivalent of a timeshare presentation. It sounds amazing in the demo. The reality is a $40k/year prison.

I'm going to say what your Salesforce AE won't: no platform does everything well. Not HubSpot. Not Salesforce. Not any of them. They do one thing well and twelve things adequately, then charge you enterprise pricing for the twelve mediocre things you didn't need.

But the real scam is the lock-in. Once your data, workflows, custom objects, and reporting all live in one platform, leaving is a 6-month project. They know this. That's the business model. Make it easy to add, impossible to leave.

I watched a 15-person sales team spend 4 months migrating to HubSpot Enterprise "to simplify things." Six months later, they have two full-time people managing the platform, their data quality is worse, and they're paying more than their previous stack of specialized tools.

Simplification was the promise. Complexity just moved. It went from visible integration work into hidden platform administration that's harder to diagnose and more expensive to fix.

Build a stack you can swap pieces in and out of. That's real operational resilience. A monolithic platform is a single point of failure dressed up as efficiency.

posts.title: Agent orchestration is the wrong layer to invest in
posts.hook: VCs are pouring money into "agent orchestration layers." I think they're investing in the wrong layer of the stack.
posts.body:
VCs are pouring money into "agent orchestration layers." I think they're investing in the wrong layer of the stack.

Orchestration assumes you have agents worth orchestrating. Most companies don't. They have one or two automations held together with duct tape and a prayer.

The bottleneck isn't coordination between agents. It's building the first agent that reliably does something useful.

I've built maybe 20 agent workflows across GTM, content, and operations. Not once has the orchestration layer been the hard part. The hard parts:

- Getting clean data into the agent
- Handling the 15% of cases where the LLM returns garbage
- Monitoring failures without drowning in alerts
- Convincing the team to actually trust the output

These are unsexy infrastructure problems. Data quality. Error handling. Observability. Trust.

The companies I'd bet on right now are the ones building better guardrails, better eval frameworks, and better data pipelines for AI workflows. Not fancier ways to chain agents together.

Orchestration becomes valuable when you have 10+ reliable agents that need to work together. Most teams are still trying to get agent number one to stop hallucinating their customer's name.

Solve reliability first. Orchestration will matter later.
posts.cta: Where are you spending most of your time when building agent workflows?

inspired_by.summary: Breaks down investment trends in AI infrastructure startups focusing on agent orchestration layers. Highlights challenges in reliability and cost for enterprise automation. Forecasts consolidation among top players in the space.

remixes.label: bottom-up-builder
remixes.hook: Everyone's talking about agent orchestration. Meanwhile, I'm still trying to get one agent to reliably parse a CSV without hallucinating column names.
remixes.body:
Everyone's talking about agent orchestration. Meanwhile, I'm still trying to get one agent to reliably parse a CSV without hallucinating column names.

This is the gap between the AI discourse and the AI reality. Conferences are full of talks about multi-agent systems, orchestration frameworks, and autonomous workflows. The people actually building agents are debugging prompt chains, handling API timeouts, and figuring out why their agent decided to skip step 3.

I've built about a dozen agent workflows over the past 6 months. Here's what I've learned: the orchestration layer is the easiest part. It's just routing. Send this output to that input, check for errors, retry if needed.

The hard part is making each individual agent node reliable. Getting an LLM to consistently extract the right fields from unstructured data. Handling the 15% of cases where the input doesn't match expected patterns. Building fallbacks for when an API is down or rate-limited.

VCs are funding the orchestration layer because it's the easiest to demo. "Look, five agents working together!" But those agents are running on carefully curated demo data where nothing goes wrong.

Put that same system against real-world messy data and watch it fall apart at the individual node level, not the orchestration level.

Build reliable single agents first. Orchestration is a solved problem. Reliability isn't.
remixes.cta: What's the most frustrating reliability issue you've hit building agent workflows?

remixes.label: vc-misalignment
remixes.hook: The AI agent market has a $2 billion mismatch between what VCs are funding and what builders actually need.
remixes.body:
The AI agent market has a $2 billion mismatch between what VCs are funding and what builders actually need.

VCs are funding orchestration layers. Platforms that coordinate multiple agents, manage handoffs, and visualize complex workflows. These are impressive products with beautiful dashboards.

Builders need reliability tools. Better error handling. Structured output validation. Graceful degradation when APIs fail. Evaluation frameworks that catch regressions before production.

This mismatch exists because orchestration sells better. "Platform for multi-agent coordination" is a pitch deck narrative. "Tool that makes a single agent fail less often" doesn't get a TechCrunch headline.

But talk to anyone shipping agents in production and they'll tell you the same thing. The bottleneck is never coordination. It's the individual agent that works 85% of the time and fails in unpredictable ways the other 15%.

I spend 80% of my agent development time on edge cases, error handling, and reliability testing for single agents. Maybe 5% on orchestration. The ratio of VC funding is almost the exact inverse.

The companies that will win this market are the ones building the unglamorous reliability layer. Output validation. Automatic fallbacks. Regression detection. The picks and shovels of agent development.

The orchestration layer is a commodity. Reliability is the moat.
remixes.cta: If you're building agents in production, what's eating most of your development time?

tones.advisor:
If you're evaluating where to invest in your AI agent stack, here's what I'd recommend: ignore the orchestration layer for now. Focus entirely on single-agent reliability.

Orchestration frameworks are appealing because they promise scale. Coordinate multiple agents, manage complex workflows, handle routing and handoffs. The vision is compelling.

But the prerequisite is having agents worth orchestrating. Most teams don't. They have one or two automations that work most of the time, with failure modes they haven't fully mapped.

I've built about a dozen agent workflows. The orchestration part, routing outputs between agents, is straightforward. Any decent engineer can build it in a day or two.

The hard part is making each individual agent node reliable enough that the orchestration layer doesn't need to constantly handle failures. That means structured output validation, graceful API fallbacks, handling malformed inputs, and regression testing.

My recommendation: build one agent that works reliably in production for 30 days. Then build a second one. Once you have 3-4 reliable agents, the orchestration layer becomes obvious and simple. You're just connecting reliable components.

Don't buy an orchestration platform before you have reliable agents. You'll end up orchestrating unreliable components, which is worse than having no orchestration at all.
tones.provocateur:
The agent orchestration hype is what happens when VCs fund demo-driven companies instead of talking to actual builders.

Nobody shipping agents in production is bottlenecked on orchestration. Not a single one. They're bottlenecked on getting a single agent to work reliably with messy real-world data.

But "multi-agent orchestration platform" makes for a great pitch deck. It demos well. You show five agents coordinating on stage, the audience claps, the term sheet follows.

Then customers buy it and discover the problem: their agents aren't reliable enough to orchestrate. The orchestration layer works perfectly. The agents underneath hallucinate, skip steps, and crash on edge cases. You've built a beautiful highway with no cars that can drive on it.

I've built more agent workflows than I can count. Want to know how much time I spend on orchestration? Almost none. It's just if/else statements and API calls. A junior developer could build it.

The time goes to: "Why did the agent extract the wrong field?" "Why does it fail on inputs with special characters?" "Why does it skip step 3 when the API returns slowly?" These are the real problems. Nobody is funding solutions to them because they're not sexy.

The agent infrastructure market has it exactly backwards. Stop funding the easy layer. Start funding the hard one.

posts.title: The career pivot nobody talks about
posts.hook: The most underrated career move in tech right now isn't learning to code. It's learning to automate your current job.
posts.body:
The most underrated career move in tech right now isn't learning to code. It's learning to automate your current job.

Every solo builder I admire follows the same pattern. They didn't start with a product idea. They started by automating their own pain.

A recruiter who built a candidate sourcing tool. A sales rep who built a prospecting engine. A content marketer who built a distribution system. Each one started by solving their own daily frustration with a script, a Clay table, an n8n workflow.

Then they realized the automation was more valuable than the job.

This is the path I took. SDR sending hundreds of emails manually, started building workflows to handle the repetitive parts, realized the workflows were the product.

You don't need to quit your job to start building. You need to start noticing which parts of your job are patterns. Every pattern is an automation opportunity. Every automation is a potential product.

The tools are free or cheap. Clay has a free tier. n8n is open source. Claude's API costs pennies per call. Supabase gives you a database for nothing.

The moat isn't the tool. It's the domain knowledge you carry from doing the work. Nobody can replicate that with a tutorial.

Start with the task you hate most. Automate it this week. See what happens.
posts.cta: What's the first thing you automated in your current role?

inspired_by.author: Sahil Lavingia
inspired_by.summary: Sahil reflected on his career pivot into tech entrepreneurship, shipping Gumroad as a solo project initially. He discussed evolving from idea to profitable business while building in public. Offered insights on minimal viable products and creator economy.

remixes.label: hidden-advantage
remixes.hook: The people building the most interesting AI tools right now aren't engineers. They're domain experts who got tired of waiting for someone to build what they needed.
remixes.body:
The people building the most interesting AI tools right now aren't engineers. They're domain experts who got tired of waiting for someone to build what they needed.

A recruiter who was manually screening 200 resumes a day built a candidate scoring tool that does the first pass in minutes. She doesn't know what a REST API is. She used Claude to write the code.

A sales manager who was spending 2 hours every morning reviewing pipeline changes built a daily brief that emails him the summary at 7am. He learned enough Python to be dangerous and automated the rest.

A content marketer who was manually posting across 4 platforms built a distribution system that schedules and publishes everywhere from one input. She started with Zapier and graduated to custom scripts.

Here's why domain experts build better tools than engineers: they know exactly where the pain is. They don't over-engineer. They don't build features nobody needs. They solve their specific problem and stop.

Engineers tend to build platforms. Domain experts build solutions. In the age of AI-assisted coding, the domain expert with a problem to solve has an unfair advantage over the engineer looking for problems to architect.

You don't need to become a developer. You need to become someone who can describe your problem clearly enough that AI helps you build the fix.
remixes.cta: What's a problem in your domain that you wish someone would just automate already?

remixes.label: automation-ladder
remixes.hook: There's a specific sequence to automating yourself into a new career, and most people start at the wrong step.
remixes.body:
There's a specific sequence to automating yourself into a new career, and most people start at the wrong step.

Most people start with: "I should learn to code." Then they take a course, build a todo app, and never connect it to their actual work.

Here's the sequence that actually works:

Step 1: Identify your daily time sinks. The repetitive tasks that eat 1-2 hours every day. Data entry, report generation, manual research, copy-pasting between tools.

Step 2: Automate one of them with whatever tool you can. Zapier, Make, a ChatGPT workflow, a simple script. Ugly is fine. It just has to save you time.

Step 3: Use the saved time to automate the next one. Each automation funds the time investment for the next.

Step 4: After 3-4 automations, you have a system. That system is your portfolio, your proof of concept, and your differentiator. You're now "the person who builds operational systems," not just "a sales rep" or "a marketer."

Step 5: Package what you built and offer it to others in your role. Every sales rep has the same pain. Every recruiter runs the same manual processes.

The career pivot happens naturally. You don't announce it. You just gradually become the person who builds things instead of the person who does things manually. The job title catches up later.
remixes.cta: What step of this sequence are you currently on?

tones.builder:
The most underrated career move in tech right now isn't learning to code. It's learning to automate your current job.

Every solo builder I admire follows the same pattern. They didn't start with a product idea. They started by automating their own pain.

A recruiter who built a candidate sourcing tool. A sales rep who built a prospecting engine. A content marketer who built a distribution system. Each one started by solving their own daily frustration with a script, a Clay table, an n8n workflow.

Then they realized the automation was more valuable than the job.

This is the path I took. SDR sending hundreds of emails manually, started building workflows to handle the repetitive parts, realized the workflows were the product.

You don't need to quit your job to start building. You need to start noticing which parts of your job are patterns. Every pattern is an automation opportunity. Every automation is a potential product.

The tools are free or cheap. Clay has a free tier. n8n is open source. Claude's API costs pennies per call. Supabase gives you a database for nothing.

The moat isn't the tool. It's the domain knowledge you carry from doing the work. Nobody can replicate that with a tutorial.

Start with the task you hate most. Automate it this week. See what happens.
tones.advisor:
If you're thinking about a career pivot, here's what I'd recommend before you enroll in a bootcamp or start learning to code from scratch.

Start with your current job. You have something most aspiring builders don't: deep domain knowledge and a clear understanding of where time gets wasted.

Every solo builder I've seen succeed followed this pattern. They didn't start with a product idea or a technology to learn. They started by automating their own pain points.

A recruiter automated candidate screening. A sales rep automated prospect research. A content marketer automated distribution. Each one started with their existing role and built outward.

The approach I recommend: spend one week documenting every repetitive task in your current role. Rank them by time consumed. Take the top one and automate it using whatever tool is accessible. Zapier, Make, Claude, a simple script. Quality doesn't matter. Saving time does.

Repeat weekly. After a month, you'll have a small system of automations that saves you hours. That system is simultaneously your portfolio, your proof of concept, and the foundation of a new career trajectory.

The key insight: you don't need to become a developer. You need to become someone who builds solutions to problems you already deeply understand. AI-assisted coding has collapsed the skill gap. Domain expertise is now the differentiator.
tones.provocateur:
"Learn to code" is the worst career advice in 2026. And coding bootcamps are still selling it because it's their entire business model.

Here's what actually moves the needle: automate your current job until you've made your role unrecognizable.

The recruiter who built a sourcing tool didn't go to a bootcamp. She got frustrated screening 200 resumes a day and asked Claude to help her build a filter. The sales rep who built a prospecting engine didn't take a CS course. He was sick of manual research and hacked together a solution over a weekend.

These people have something no bootcamp graduate has: 5,000+ hours of domain expertise telling them exactly what to build and exactly what "good" looks like.

A developer can build you a candidate screening tool. But they'll over-engineer it, add features you don't need, and miss the nuances that only someone who's screened 10,000 resumes would catch.

The career pivot nobody talks about isn't technical. It's operational. Stop being the person who does the work manually. Start being the person who builds the system that does the work.

You don't need to learn Python. You need to learn to describe your problem clearly enough that AI writes the Python for you. That's a two-week skill, not a six-month bootcamp.

platforms.newsletter:
I've been thinking about career pivots and wanted to share a pattern I keep seeing.

Every solo builder I admire followed the same path. They didn't start with a product idea or a bootcamp enrollment. They started by looking at their existing job and asking: "What am I doing manually that a machine should handle?"

A recruiter built candidate screening. A sales rep built prospect research. A content marketer built distribution. Each started by automating their own pain, and the career pivot happened as a side effect.

The insight that changed my perspective: in 2026, domain expertise is more valuable than coding skill. AI can write the code. It can't tell you what to build or whether the output is actually good. That judgment comes from years of doing the work yourself.

If you're considering a pivot, don't start with technology. Start with your biggest daily time sink.
